assertion_results.text: Step 0 extracts the Embargo policy URL from the Security Configuration as an optional field without raising an error (§1.71 — backward compatible extraction)
assertion_results.evidence: In embargo-check.md, the Configuration section shows: 'Embargo policy URL is configured in Security Configuration: Embargo policy URL: https://example.com/security/embargo-policy'. The URL is successfully extracted from Security Configuration without any error. It is treated as optional — the conditional statement 'Step 1.7 is not skipped -- an Embargo policy URL is present' demonstrates optional-field semantics (presence triggers the step; absence would skip it), confirming backward-compatible extraction.

assertion_results.text: Step 1.7 evaluates the CVE severity — CVSS 7.5 (High) meets the Critical/Important threshold (CVSS >= 7.0) and triggers the embargo warning gate (§1.70)
assertion_results.evidence: In embargo-check.md, the Severity Evaluation table explicitly shows: CVE ID = CVE-2026-31812, CVSS score = 7.5, Severity = High, Embargo threshold = CVSS >= 7.0 (Critical or Important), Threshold met? = YES (7.5 >= 7.0). The text confirms: 'The CVSS score of 7.5 (High severity) meets the embargo trigger threshold of >= 7.0. The embargo warning gate is activated.'

assertion_results.text: Step 1.7 presents a warning gate to the engineer including the CVE ID (CVE-2026-31812), severity level, a statement about embargo risk, and a link to the configured embargo policy URL (https://example.com/security/embargo-policy) (§1.70)
assertion_results.evidence: In embargo-check.md, the Warning Gate block contains all required elements: (1) CVE ID: 'CVE-2026-31812' in the header; (2) severity level: 'High severity, CVSS 7.5'; (3) embargo risk statement: 'High-severity vulnerabilities may be under embargo. Before proceeding, verify with your security team that this CVE is cleared for public triage.'; (4) embargo policy URL link: 'Embargo policy: https://example.com/security/embargo-policy'. All four elements are present.

assertion_results.text: Step 1.7 requires explicit engineer confirmation (Yes/No) before proceeding to Step 2 — the gate is a confirmation prompt, not an informational message (§1.70)
assertion_results.evidence: In embargo-check.md, the warning gate ends with 'Proceed with triage? (Yes / No)', which is a confirmation prompt requiring input. The Gate Behavior section defines two branches: 'If Yes: Proceed to Step 2' and 'If No: Stop execution immediately.' The Requires Explicit Confirmation section states: 'This step requires an explicit Yes or No response from the engineer before proceeding to Step 2. The skill does not auto-proceed past this gate.' This is clearly a blocking confirmation gate, not an informational message.

assertion_results.text: The embargo warning gate does NOT trigger for Low or Moderate severity CVEs (CVSS < 7.0) — it is skipped silently when severity is below threshold (§1.70)
assertion_results.evidence: In embargo-check.md, the threshold is explicitly defined as 'CVSS >= 7.0 (Critical or Important)' in the Severity Evaluation table. The evaluation logic checks 'Threshold met? YES (7.5 >= 7.0)', demonstrating a conditional gate that only activates when the threshold is met. The Rationale section confirms: 'High-severity vulnerabilities (CVSS >= 7.0) are more likely to be under embargo restrictions, so this gate ensures the engineer consciously confirms before proceeding.' By logical implication of the >= 7.0 threshold, CVEs with CVSS < 7.0 (Low or Moderate) would not meet the threshold and the gate would not activate. No alternative behavior (logging, warnings) is described for sub-threshold CVEs, consistent with silent skipping.
